text: the ghost of the girl I was before. To her anger. There is no protection here. Already, it has begun its absorption of you, and I could take you away from here. Go to a hotel if they would take us. But you are weak from not eating. Susceptible. I can’t deliver you from one death into another, and how do you banish a ghost that never died? How do you exorcise evil created from a fervency of belief? “Please,” I say, and there is no magic in that word. How many times did it pass my lips even as my father sweated red-faced over me, my body breaking under his version of justice? From upstairs, I hear you call my name, and my ghost turns toward the sound and vanishes. I do scream then as I scramble to my feet, stumble over the salt, the candles, and run toward the stairs. I call your name, but all is quiet and still. The air is oppressive and pushes against me as I reach the top and call your name. You don’t answer, and the silence is an awful sort of violence. I would take you into me, wrap you in the meat of my body and keep you safe. Anything to keep you with me. Anything. Ahead of me our bedroom door opens. The darkness inside shifting like something alive, and I force myself on even though I am shaking because you are inside with it. With the anger and longing I tried to leave behind. But intentions always leave a residue, don’t they? We cannot erase who we were. It is a dormant beast. Again I call your name as I enter our room. You are sitting in the middle of our bed, the blankets nested around you, and my chest aches with how beautiful you are. “What are you doing?” you ask, and your eyes are huge. Luminous in the dark. Like my ghost. “Get out of her.” I clench my hands into fists, but my hands have always been weak. I have never been able to fight back. Your throat and chest swell impossibly, the skin stretching to make room for what should not be, and I let out a sob. Too late. Always too late. My life has been a sum of all the ways a person can be powerless. But you are mine. Our vows etched on my skin, thrumming in my blood, and I am the wailing in the night. The teeth in the dark. You are meant only for me. I kneel over you, and your eyes widen. “What are you doing?” you ask again. There is panic in your voice, but it’s your voice, and I kiss you once. “I have to. You don’t understand.” “You don’t. It’s not—” The knife I still carry is sharp. You part under me easily as I cut myself out of you. You scream, your hands beating against me, but I won’t let it have you. Around me, the house shudders, and the room expands and contracts like the chamber of some terrible heart. My hands are slick with you, but the air is clear. My ghost purged. I touch your face, your hair, your mouth, wait for you to draw me to you, but your hands are limp at your sides. Your body still. “No,” I say. If my ghost were still here, it would laugh. I place my mouth over yours, but your lungs won’t take my air, and I howl as I claw at your chest, as I scream your name into the emptied house. I reach into the thin cage of your chest and touch the heart you gave me. Your body is my body. Your blood is my blood. And there are teeth, and teeth, and teeth. I take you into me, and we are bound in this way. A communion of blood and flesh. I can only hope, even as I swallow your heart, that you will haunt me. Please. Haunt me. Robert E. Howard's footprint on horror fiction in particular and dark speculative fiction in general is large and imposing, and one of the things I've always loved about his work is the slippery line he treads between horror and fantasy, creating some of the most enduring horror-fantasy stories ever written. Especially his horror/weird westerns. With “O Adelín,” Michael Wehunt brings his own darkly magical horror/weird western, which isn't surprising, given his places in the pages of Year's Best Dark Fantasy and Horror and Year's Best Horror Fiction. I truly hope this won't be the only time he graces the pages of Liminal Spaces. O Adelín by Michael Wehunt W e formed a broken circle around the fire. In the empty space sat a black glass stone, proxy for the blood man. Its surface flickered oily red. The night was too warm for fire but we desired its light and its invitation to commune closer than most would. Eleven of us aged mercenaries had set out over the little mountains bound for El Porvenir and six remained. We sipped sour mash from tin cups. Nearby the horses trembled and showed their teeth. For luck we had yet to name them. The cart groaned under the weight of bison skins, among them the bounty heads of a rogue capitán and two of his men. The low drone of flies stitched the air. Wind soughed through the stand of ash trees in which we made our camp and all eyes were averted from the strange stars glittering down at us. The moon had shrunk from a crescent rind into nothing for days. We kept our weapons near to hand. Our voices were scant murmurs punctuated by the ruecrows roosting black in the trees calling out their omens. The city lay some five miles distant on the cracked desert below the mountains. It looked like something a man could pinch between two fingers and rub against a boot heel. Mud houses atop
